retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The wettability of the impression material determines its ability to record fine details, which depends on its viscosity. The more the impression material wets the tissues, the more it comes in close contact with them and captures fine details. The more viscous materials have limited flow and very few wetting features. [4] In fixed dental prostheses, an impression is expected to reproduce 20 to 70 microns, and 100 to 150 microns in removable prosthodontics. According to international standards, the impression material should record a line of 0.02 mm width or less, which is less than the width of a human hair. [5] Impression Materials Classification: 1. Based on use: For primary impressions, eg, alginate, impression compound, and elastomeric putty. For secondary impressions, eg, light body elastomers and zinc oxide eugenol. 2. Based on the setting reaction:

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Dustless alginates: manufactured to prevent inhalation of alginate particles, which may cause respiratory problems. The alginate particles are coated with glycol or glycerine to make them denser. Chromatic alginates: change colors during manipulation to guide mixing time and setting time. In the form of sol or paste (containing water) and a reactor (plaster of Paris) separately. Agar Agar is another hydrophilic colloid impression material like alginate, but it is reversible. It is the first elastic impression material to be used in dentistry. 1. Composition: Chemically, agar is a sulfuric ester of the galactan complex, and agar is available as a tray or syringe material. Agar (gelling agent) Borax (strengthener) Potassium sulfate (gypsum hardener) Alkyl benzoate (preservatives) Water (reaction medium > 80%) Fillers, flavoring, and coloring agents

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Reversible (physical): impression compound, impression waxes, agar. Irreversible (chemical): alginate, impression plaster, elastomeric impression materials. 3. Based on the state of the impression material after setting in the oral cavity: Rigid: impression compound, zinc oxide eugenol, impression plaster, impression waxes. Elastic: alginate, agar, polysulfides, addition and condensation silicones, polyether. 4. Based on compression of underlying tissues: Mucostatic: impression plaster, light body elastomers. Mucocompressive: impression compound, putty, and elastomers. 5. Based on consistencies: Light body Medium body Heavy body Putty

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Wax was the only impression material used in dentistry until the mid-19th century, when gutta-percha first appeared. Then, in 1857, Charles Stent created a thermoplastic modeling compound similar to today's impression compound. Still, the problem with this material was that it was rigid and could not reproduce undercut areas. All the impression materials used until that date became rigid after setting and could not copy the oral tissues accurately. Thus, there was always a need for an impression material that could remain elastic even after setting. That is when agar, a reversible hydrocolloid manufactured from algae, was introduced in dentistry. Although this jelly-like material was elastic, it required a complicated procedure to be used as an impression material.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Technique or Treatment -- Disinfection of Impressions
retrieved_docs.content: Alginates are usually disinfected during the preparation step by spraying or mixing the alginate powder with a disinfectant solution instead of water, allowing the impression to be poured immediately. [14] However, mixing alginate with disinfectant solutions may affect its properties, depending on the type and concentration of the disinfectant agent. [10] Studies indicate that chlorhexidine is the most suitable disinfection agent to mix with alginate as it did not significantly alter the properties of the material. [10] Disinfected gypsum die stones may also be used, avoiding the distortion experienced by the impression material during other disinfection maneuvers. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Impression compound is a thermoplastic, rigid, and reversible impression material (meaning it can be reused in the same patient if an impression needs to be repeated). It is also known as a modeling compound. See Image. Impression Compound. 1. Types of impression compound Type 1: Low-fusing impression compound. Available as a sheet, rope, and stick. The sheet material impresses edentulous arches, and the stick form is used for border molding. Type 2: High-fusing impression compound, also known as tray compound. It is used for making impression trays. 2. Composition of impression compound Rosin 30 parts Resin 30 parts Carnauba wax 10 parts Stearic acid 5parts (gives plasticity) Talc 75parts (provides hardness) Coloring agents 3. Setting reaction: a thermoplastic material that sets by physical reaction only. 4. Instruments and armamentarium: water bath, gauge piece, burner, scalpel handle, and blade to modify the impression.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: 3. Composition: The base contains polysulfide prepolymer, filler for strength, plasticizer for optimum consistency, and sulfur as an accelerator 4. The catalyst contains lead oxide (gives a characteristic brown color), fillers, plasticizers, and retarders

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Zinc oxide eugenol (ZOE) impression material is an irreversible nonelastic material used as a final impression (wash impression) in complete denture fabrication. It records the tissues in the undistorted state. [9] [10] 1. Composition: ZOE impression material is available in 2 collapsible tubes; 2 contain the base and the other the catalyst (see Image . Zinc Oxide Eugenol). The base contains ZnO 87%, vegetable or mineral oils 13%. The catalyst contains eugenol 12-15%, rosin 50%, fillers 20%, resinous balsam 10%, accelerator, and color pigments 5%. *Noneugenol pastes (for patients who are allergic to eugenol) contain carboxylic acids. 2. Instruments and armamentarium: paper pad or glass slab and broad-end spatula. 3. Manipulation: equal lengths are squeezed out from both tubes on a paper pad or a glass slab and mixed with a broad-end spatula until they are mixed to an even color.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Properties
retrieved_docs.content: Alginates are mucostatic impression materials. When freshly spatulated, alginates have low viscosity and record the soft tissues without compressing them. Alginates are easy to manipulate, affordable, and have rapid setting times. [3] Some dental alginates have added features such as the ability to change color to signal the phases of the chemical reaction and different flavors. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Alginate is a hydrocolloid widely used as an impression material in dentistry. It is derived from alginic acid, present in brown algae. It is an irreversible and elastic impression material. [13] See Image. Alginate, Impression Making. 1. Composition: Sodium or potassium alginate (15%) - dissolves in water and forms a viscous sol Calcium sulfate (16%) - reactor Diatomaceous earth (60%) - filler Zinc oxide (4%) - filler Potassium titanium fluoride (3%) - accelerator Sodium phosphate (2%) - retarder Glycol (in dust-free alginates) Pigments and flavoring agents 2. Indications: Diagnostic casts for treatment planning Orthodontic study models Impressions to fabricate partial and complete dentures, and temporomandibular disorder appliances For duplicating models 3. Instruments and armamentarium: flexible rubber bowl, curved spatula, measuring scoop, perforated impression tray, alginate powder, and water.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: When the algae used to manufacture agar were unavailable during the Second World War, Americans used local algae to manufacture another elastic impression material known as alginate, which has gained popularity since then. Alginate and agar have disadvantages, like dimensional instability and low tear strength, which led to the manufacture of elastomeric (also known as rubber-based) impression materials. First came polysulfide, then condensation silicon, followed by polyether, and then addition silicones. [1] [2] With the advancement in technology, digital dentistry is also making its way into the field. [3] However, there is always a scope for new impression materials, as no impression material is 100% accurate until the date.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Impressions must be thoroughly disinfected to prevent cross-infections. After washing the impression to remove residues of saliva and blood, the chosen disinfectant is applied, either by spraying on the impression or immersing it in the disinfectant solution for a specified time. Hydrocolloids (agar and alginate) and polyethers possess hydrophilic features. Therefore, if disinfection guidelines are not followed, it may lead to swelling of the impressions, but when done properly, it does not affect the dimensional stability and accuracy of the impression. Apart from being used as impression materials, elastomers are also used as permanent soft denture liners in constantly abused tissues. [31] Elastomers and zinc oxide eugenol are used as bite registration materials. [32] [12] The dentist has access to various impression materials and should be aware of their properties, manipulation, and indications, considering their limitations.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Impression materials are classified as either elastic or non-elastic based on mechanical properties. [1] Elastic materials are capable of stretching, compressing, and recovering after deformation. Elastic materials comprise reversible and irreversible hydrocolloids, addition and condensation silicones, polysulfides, and polyether. [2] Nonelastic or rigid impression materials include impression compounds, zinc oxide eugenol, and impression waxes. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Technique or Treatment -- Disinfection of Impressions
retrieved_docs.content: The first step in disinfecting impressions is to rinse them well with tap water, which removes a significant number of microorganisms from the impression. [5] The most effective method for disinfecting an impression is by immersing it in a disinfectant liquid for 30 minutes. [14] This is more advantageous for hydrophobic materials because they only experience a slight distortion when disinfected this way. [14] By contrast, irreversible hydrocolloids are hydrophilic materials and undergo distortion when immersed in a solution.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Dentists must select the impression material according to the patient's therapeutic needs. The materials used for primary impressions for removable complete dentures include alginate, impression compound, and elastomers in putty consistency. [29] [6] In secondary impressions, zinc oxide eugenol and light body elastomers are indicated. [30] Alginate and elastomers are used to impress the oral tissues in partial removable and fixed dentures. Addition-cured silicones and polyethers are the most commonly used impression materials in implant prosthodontics.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: 3. Composition: The base contains hydroxy-terminated polydimethylsiloxane, which forms a polymer, and fillers that control viscosity. The accelerator contains tetraethyl silicate and tin octoate (catalyst) [25] 4. Setting reaction: Condensation polymerization reaction occurs with the release of alcohol as a byproduct that evaporates as it is formed. 5. Advantages: Good elastic recovery Pleasant odor and taste Accurate reproduction 6. Disadvantages: Dimensional deformation over time Hydrophobic Dimensional stability is affected due to reaction byproducts [25] Delivery Systems of Elastomers

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 888
retrieved_docs.content: Organic nitrogen compounds are a class of chemical entities that share a structural relationship with pyridine or pyridine derivatives, indicating that pyridine serves as a parent scaffold for many members of this group. Specific drugs that are classified as organic nitrogen compounds include pentazocin, the antidepressant mianserin, and its salt form mianserin hydrochloride, each of which inherits the pyridine‑based core. Additionally, dihydralazin is recognized as an organic nitrogen compound, reinforcing the broader inclusion of diverse pharmacologically active agents within this chemical class. The common pyridine ancestry of these compounds underlies shared physicochemical characteristics and contributes to their therapeutic actions, highlighting the importance of the pyridine scaffold in the design and classification of organic nitrogen–containing medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2527
retrieved_docs.content: Isopropamide iodide is categorized as a bulk compounding chemical, and bulk compounding chemicals are classified within the broader chemical group of tricalcium phosphate, linking the specific compound to its larger chemical family.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1431
retrieved_docs.content: Karbenoxolon and ingenol mebutate are both classified as terpenes, indicating that each compound belongs to the large family of isoprene‑derived natural products. As members of the terpene class, they share characteristic structural features that influence their pharmacological properties and potential therapeutic applications. Recognizing both karbenoxolon and ingenol mebutate as terpenes aids clinicians and researchers in understanding their mechanisms of action, predicting biological effects, and guiding drug development and patient‑care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1562
retrieved_docs.content: Ibopamine is classified as a substance with phenol structure, indicating that it belongs to the broader category of phenolic compounds characterized by a hydroxyl group attached to an aromatic ring. Recognizing ibopamine as a phenolic substance informs its pharmacological properties and potential interactions within medical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1373
retrieved_docs.content: Alkoholy is a broad chemical category that encompasses both chlorbutanol and heptaminol, indicating that these two compounds are classified as types of alkoholy. Recognizing chlorbutanol and heptaminol as members of the alkoholy class helps clarify their shared chemical characteristics and informs their pharmacological profiling and potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 326
retrieved_docs.content: sodium chloride (²²Na), indicating a broader chemical relationship that may be relevant for tracer studies or specialized diagnostic applications. Overall, the presence of chlorid sodný across these diverse products underscores its dual role as both a critical excipient that ensures proper formulation characteristics and, in certain multi‑component preparations, an active therapeutic ingredient contributing to the intended pharmacological effect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1978
retrieved_docs.content: Heptaminol is classified as an alkoholy, indicating that it belongs to the broader category of alkoholy substances. This relationship places heptaminol within the alkoholy class, linking its pharmacologic profile to the characteristics associated with alkoholy compounds.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2566
retrieved_docs.content: Medicinal product categorized by structure is a classification that encompasses several specific drugs, including midodrin, modafinil, isoaminile, and pirenzepin. By grouping these agents under a common structural category, clinicians and researchers can better understand their pharmacologic characteristics, predict therapeutic effects, and make informed decisions about drug selection and patient management. This structural categorization supports systematic drug development, facilitates comparison of related compounds, and aids in optimizing treatment strategies based on the chemical properties shared by midodrin, modafinil, isoaminile, and pirenzepin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 939
retrieved_docs.content: Crofelemer is classified as a substance that possesses a phenol structure, situating it within the broader category of phenolic compounds and underscoring the chemical characteristics that influence its pharmacologic properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 4 -- Community 2797
retrieved_docs.content: The chemical entity described as a “carboxylic acid and/or carboxylic acid derivative‑containing product” is classified as a tetrakain, indicating that it belongs to the tetrakain class of compounds. In addition, the agent ingenium mebutate is regarded as a specific instance or subclass within the broader category of carboxylic acid and/or carboxylic acid derivative‑containing products, meaning that ingenium mebutate inherits the chemical characteristics of this product class, which itself is a type of tetrakain.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1699
retrieved_docs.content: Capsaicin and related agents are classified as members of the zucapsaicin group, indicating that they share common chemical and pharmacologic characteristics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2040
retrieved_docs.content: identified as an opioid analgesic, highlighting the therapeutic potential of kappa‑receptor targeting agents, which may offer analgesia with a different side‑effect profile compared with traditional mu‑agonists. Collectively, these entities demonstrate that opioid analgesics encompass a broad spectrum of dosage forms (injectable, oral solution, capsule), combination products, and both classic and investigational opioid molecules, all aimed at providing effective pain control across various clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: the diluent. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate how specific active molecules, their parent or precursor compounds, and formulation excipients are organized within pharmacological classes and therapeutic products, providing a comprehensive view of their chemical taxonomy, mechanisms of action, and clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2558
retrieved_docs.content: Buprenorfin is classified as a naturally occurring alkaloid, indicating that it is a bioactive chemical compound derived from natural sources with the typical structural features of alkaloids. It is also identified as a plant agent product, emphasizing its origin from botanical material and its role as a plant‑derived substance. Additionally, buprenorfin is described as a phytonutrient, suggesting that it possesses nutritional or therapeutic properties associated with plant‑based nutrients. Recognizing buprenorfin’s overlapping identities as an alkaloid, a plant‑derived product, and a phytonutrient provides a comprehensive understanding of its potential pharmacological effects and its relevance in both medicinal and nutritional contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1310
retrieved_docs.content: Tilidine is classified as a carboxylic acid and/or carboxylic acid derivative‑containing product, meaning it belongs to the group of compounds that incorporate a carboxyl functional group or its derivatives. This chemical classification informs its pharmacological characteristics, metabolism, and potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1269
retrieved_docs.content: Antipropulsives are classified as members of the opium group, indicating that these agents are derived from or chemically related to opium and share its pharmacologic characteristics.
